coredata.dc:title: In-situ One-Pot Novel Synthesis of Molybdenum di-Telluride@Carbon Nano-Dots for Sensitive and Selective Detection of Hydrogen Peroxide Molecules via Turn-off Fluorescence Mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Sagar, Pinky
Srivastava, Monika
Tiwari, Rajan Kumar
Kumar, Ajay
Srivastava, Amit
Pandey, Gajanan
Srivastava, S.K.
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) based materials have grabbed striking attention to a great extent owing to their intriguing characteristics for biological, optical, chemical and medicinal applications. Herein, we report in-situ one-pot hydrothermal-synthesis of molybdenum ditelluride@carbon nano-dots (MoTe2@C NDs) and then explored its potency in biosensing. The MoTe2@C NDs have been characterized by using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) for confirmation. Furthermore, the photophysical properties of MoTe2@C NDs have been examined. Under radiation of Ultra-Violet (UV) light (λ = 365 nm), the MoTe2@C NDs emit cyan fluorescence which has been confirmed by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (PL peak at ∼431 nm, excitation wavelength ∼350 nm). The as-synthesized sample has been subjugated for the sensing of hydrogen-peroxide (H2O2) molecules. Notably, with a gradual proliferation of the concentration of H2O2, the PL intensity of the MoTe2@C NDs decreases and is found to be linear (R2 = 0.9986). The detection-limit has also been estimated as 14.22 nM. The PL quenching-mechanism has been elucidated based on three-level kinetic model considering charge separated-trap states (1[D+···A−] ↔ 3[D+···A−]). This is the first work of hydrothermal-synthesis of MoTe2@C NDs and study of its optical properties, which has efficient potential to use as H2O2 sensor in industrial/biomedical applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Core- level XPS-spectra of MoTe2@C NDs containing (a) Mo 3d, (b) Te 3d, (c) C 1s and (d) O 1s elements.
(a) TEM image (bar scale: 50 nm), (inset) particle-size distribution profile of MoTe2@C (b) HR-TEM image (bar scale: 10 nm), (c) HR-TEM image of single MoTe2@C ND (bar scale: 1 nm), (d) SAED pattern.
(a) optical UV–visible absorption (blue curve), PL excitation (black curve, λem = 431) and PL emission (red curve, λex = 350) and (b) excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra.
(a) PL spectra upon gradual addition of H2O2 to the solution of MoTe2@C NDs in DI water (n = 3), (b) corresponding linear fit S-V plot.

(a) A generalized Jablonski-diagram showing charge separation and stabilization between S-T charge separated states along with a trap-state. (b) Fluorescence-quenching of MoTe2@C NDs upon addition of H2O2 using generalized three-level kinetic model.
A comparison of MoTe2@C NDs with earlier reported fluorescence based H2O2 sensing probes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) based materials have grabbed striking attention to a great extent owing to their intriguing characteristics for biological, optical, chemical and medicinal applications. Herein, we report in-situ one-pot hydrothermal-synthesis of molybdenum ditelluride@carbon nano-dots (MoTe2@C NDs) and then explored its potency in biosensing. The MoTe2@C NDs have been characterized by using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) for confirmation. Furthermore, the photophysical properties of MoTe2@C NDs have been examined. Under radiation of Ultra-Violet (UV) light (λ = 365 nm), the MoTe2@C NDs emit cyan fluorescence which has been confirmed by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (PL peak at ∼431 nm, excitation wavelength ∼350 nm). The as-synthesized sample has been subjugated for the sensing of hydrogen-peroxide (H2O2) molecules. Notably, with a gradual proliferation of the concentration of H2O2, the PL intensity of the MoTe2@C NDs decreases and is found to be linear (R2 = 0.9986). The detection-limit has also been estimated as 14.22 nM. The PL quenching-mechanism has been elucidated based on three-level kinetic model considering charge separated-trap states (1[D+···A−] ↔ 3[D+···A−]). This is the first work of hydrothermal-synthesis of MoTe2@C NDs and study of its optical properties, which has efficient potential to use as H2O2 sensor in industrial/biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) TMDs have drawn attention widely in the past few years owing to their intrinsic prominent properties such as catalytic, electronic, mechanical, and optical properties, after the graphene and graphene-like materials [1–4]. To date, several 2D TMDs have been synthesized including MoS2, MoSe2, MoTe2, WS2, WSe2, etc. as mono/few-layered, thin-films, nanocrystals (NCs), and QDs, etc [5–10]. Despite the promising practice of these materials, composites have been considered to a great extent in previous research works [11–13]. Among group VI- TMDs, MoTe2 are known for their high aspect ratio, tremendous fluorescent properties, chemical-stability, solubility in aqueous-media, and other interesting features [14]. The polymorphism (two different kinds of symmetries) nature of the MoTe2 makes it a block of intense research interest [15]. Moreover, the most stable and commonly studied phase of MoTe2 has a hexagonal phase. In the 2H-phase, each MoTe2 monolayer has been formed by a layer of hexagonally arranged Mo (transition-metal) atoms sandwiched between layers of Te (chalcogen) atoms [2,14]. These layers are bound with each other via weak van der Waals forces [2,16]. In contrast to other TMDs, MoTe2 can be synthesized directly in semiconducting as well as metallic phases [15,17]. MoTe2 possesses remarkable semiconducting characteristics that offers unique opportunities in various fields like optoelectronics, spintronics, etc [15,18,19]. Structurally, MoTe2 are very similar to graphite and can be a pronounced material of interest as sensors, photodetectors, batteries, energy storage materials, other electrochemical devices, and so forth because of its peculiar electrical, chemical, optical and catalytic properties [20–22]. Recently, Cui et al. have reported asymmetric spin–orbit coupling in 1Td-MoTe2 as transport evidence [23]. Wang et al. demonstrated structural phase change in monolayer MoTe2 via electrostatic doping [16]. Also, Huang et al. developed a ferroelectric polymer tuned photodetector with the help of 2D layered MoTe2 [24]. These reports suggest that the works on MoTe2 have been explored mostly dealing with the electronic and optoelectronic features of MoTe2. To date, innumerable synthesis protocols have been delineated for synthesizing MoTe2 nanomaterial, in which chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is the most widely adopted [1,8]. This approach is well suitable for the production of 2D layered materials but it comprises lots of tedious and time-consuming steps [2]. Besides, there is yet no evidence of a MoTe2@C NDs incorporating eco-friendly hydrothermal-method towards a systematic analysis employing optical properties in the field of biosensing.

Even large-number of studies of fluorescent properties explored on NDs, fluorescence mechanism yet a hot-topic among researchers [25]. NDs synthesized by chemical-method comprise a core and outer-surface which is extremely rich in functional moieties. The core-part of the NDs can be considered as tiny-sheets of MoTe2 (stacked by weak van der Waals interactions), on the other hand outer surface consists of carbon rich various functional-groups which contain some molecular-fluorophores [26–28]. On the other hand, it is experimentally proven that the fluorescence characteristics is a combination of core and surface functional-groups, and also highly depends upon the adopted synthesis route [26,28–30].

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is vastly used chemical in various industrial processes such as paper and pulp manufacturing process, environment cleaning, medical and food processing in form of bleaching agent, green solvent, and disinfectant, etc [31]. Apart from it, H2O2 is a form of most reactive oxygen species (ROS) [32]. It plays a vital role as co-product in cell metabolism, enzyme-catalyzation (horseradish peroxidase, glucose oxidase etc.), common biomarker and several biological reactions. It is well-known for its cytotoxicity to cellular life and an indicator to oxidative stress. Hence, wide production and gathering of H2O2 in biological lives can grow oxidative stress to living systems that can cause cardiovascular disease, arthritis, neurodegenerative, aging, tumor etc. for these reasons, residuals of H2O2 in food as well as health systems must be effectually controlled [33,34]. Many countries have already challenged the use of H2O2 in controlled way such as FDA regulation imposed a limit on H2O2 levels to 0.5 ppm in finalized food wrapping. There are various analytical techniques are developed for the qualitative determination of H2O2 which includes spectrophotometry, colorimetry, electrochemical, fluorometry, chemiluminescence [31,35–39]. Fluorescence based sensors are conventional, cheap, easy to handle, rapid and in situ operational which makes them superior to be used as sensing technique on a vast level. These techniques are highly sensitive, selective and requires very small amount of analyte unlike traditional spectrophotometric techniques [40,41].

In the present study, to the best of our knowledge, MoTe2@C NDs have been synthesized for the very first time by engrossing the ‘hydrothermal-approach’. The formation MoTe2@C NDs has been confirmed via XPS, TEM, XRD (X-ray diffraction) and Raman-analysis followed by UV–visible and PL spectroscopy. Moreover, the fluorescence-based sensing experiment has been executed for the detection of the H2O2 and found that with a gradual proliferation in the concentration of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs decreases and is found to be linear (R2 = 0.9986). The fluorescence-quenching mechanism was elucidated via opting three-level-kinetic model earlier reported by Kalytchuk et al [42]. Henceforth, absorption, fluorescence and life time fluorescence studies of as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs with the addition of H2O2 and the subsequent ensuing mechanism have been detailed and explained explicitly.
All the information related to materials, instruments and synthesis (detailed, Fig. S1), cell viability, cytotoxicity and cell imaging were incorporated in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS, Raman, XRD, TEM and EDX analysis of MoTe2@C NDs
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to synthesize MoTe2@C NDs one-hand facile hydrothermal-method has been opted; schematically illustrated in Fig. S1. Next, the chemical environment of the MoTe2@C NDs have been explored via opting XPS. The XPS-analysis describes the presence of Mo (molybdenum), Te (tellurium), O (oxygen), and C (carbon) in the MoTe2@C NDs, as shown in Fig. 1 which is further authenticated by the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. From Fig. 1(a), Mo 3d spectrum shows the appearance of two peaks at ∼231.0 eV (Mo 3d5/2) and ∼234.0 eV (Mo 3d3/2) corresponding to Mo-Te bonds. Similarly, Te 3d spectrum (Fig. 1(b)) exhibits peaks at ∼575.6 eV (Te 3d5/2) and ∼588.2 eV (Te 3d3/2) attributing to the Mo-Te bonds [13,21]. In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(c)), peaks at ∼284.8, 286.5, 287.6 and 289.0 eV are dedicated to sp2 C (which is adventitious) (CC), COH, CO and OCO, respectively [13,43,44]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(d)) exhibits three peaks appeared at ∼529.3 eV, 530.8 eV and 532.4 eV referring to the presence of metallic-O bonds, hydroxyl-groups and organic CO, respectively [13,45,46]. The core-level spectrum of MoTe2@C NDs signifies the presence of Mo and Te in +4 and −2 oxidation states, respectively, giving the sufficient evidence to form MoTe2 compound [13,47].

In Raman spectrum Fig. S2(a), the characteristic signals at lower wavenumber appeared around 125 and 286 cm−1 correspond to Ag mode, while signal at ∼190 cm−1 appeared due to B2g mode of hexagonal MoTe2 which are consistent with previously reported works [13,47,48]. Here, Ag and B2g represent out of plane vibration for MoTe2. Moreover, the observed features are well indexed with the 2-H semiconducting phase of the MoTe2. Apart from it, several other peaks are also appeared in the spectrum which are probably due to the second order Raman scattering bearing much weaker intensity than first order Raman scattering signals [49]. Besides, in our case, we have measured weak signals of the Raman spectrum than the previous studies for bulk MoTe2. This careful observation can be due to the absence of strong van der Waals forces and adsorption of solvents to the surface of NDs. The presence of B2g mode can be ascribed to the formation of ultra-thin sized nanoparticle because B2g mode is inactive in case of monolayer and bulk 2H-MoTe2 [49,50].

Furthermore, the structural phase of MoTe2@C NDs has been investigated by XRD-analysis as illustrated in Fig. S2(b). Peaks located at (2θ∼) 33.17° corresponds to hexagonal crystalline phase of MoTe2 (JCPDS card No. 15-0658) [13,47]. On the contrary, typical peaks (2θ = ) 27.5° reflect the presence of carbonaceous material [13]. In present study, a distinct amorphous like background has been found with no differentiable XRD signals. This result can be attributed to the presence of various functional groups existing over the surface of MoTe2@C NDs and is in good agreement of several earlier reported works [25,51].

The morphology of the as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs has been analyzed by TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 (a), (b) and (c). Fig. 2(a) and (b) reveal the spherical-shape of MoTe2@C NDs. The average particle size of as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs is found as ∼5.5 nm. The respective size distribution profile is displayed in the inset of Fig. 2(a). HR-TEM images of MoTe2@C NDs are shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b), where, Fig. 2(b) depicts the lattice fringe spacing of 0.22 nm. Also, SAED pattern (Fig. 2(d)) unveils the amorphous nature of the as-synthesized material which is in good agreement of earlier reported NDs [52]. Moreover, EDX-analysis corroborates the presence of elements Mo, Te, C and O, spectrum is shown in Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DLS and Zeta potential analysis of MoTe2@C NDs
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of various functional groups present over the surface of MoTe2@C NDs was supported by DLS and Zeta potential measurements. DLS investigations shows that MoTe2@C NDs have self-assembling capability, see Fig. S4(a) [53]. On the other hand, Zeta potential measurement (Fig. S4(b)), reveals the surface potential values of −25.7 mV for MoTe2@C NDs. Henceforth, the results suggest that aggregates of NDs are formed in the solvent [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-physical properties (UV–visible absorption, PL and FTIR spectroscopy) of MoTe2@C NDs
ce:section.ce:para: To inquest, the photo-physical properties of as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs, 5 µL of MoTe2@C NDs has been dissolved in 2.75 mL DI water in quartz cuvette. The UV-absorption spectrum for the as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs, as shown in Fig. 3 (a) illustrates a prominent peak at 264 nm when scanned from 700–190 nm. The prominent peak positioned at ∼264 nm can be recognized as the surface absorbed functional-groups (OH, OCO) while some weak absorption bands have also been identified at 326 nm, 427 nm and 602 nm correspond to the transitions in the different sections of the Brillouin zone of the MoTe2 (Fig. S5(a)). The low intense absorption bands are the signal towards the formation of MoTe2-NDs with contracted lateral dimension as compared to 2D thin films of MoTe2 which can be realized as the quantum-confinement effect [29,41,55,56]. The bandgap of MoTe2@C NDs has been evaluated via Tauc’s plot (Fig. S5(b)) and estimated as ∼3.938 eV, which is higher than the reported for the bulk MoTe2 (1.0–1.1 eV) [24,57]. Under the illumination of UV lamp (λ = 365 nm), MoTe2@C NDs solution exhibit cyan fluorescence (Fig. S6(a)). Fig. 3(a) traces out the PL excitation and emission profiles also describing PL intensity at 431 nm for 350 nm excitation. Besides, the as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs show excitation-wavelength (λex) dependent PL behavior as represented in Fig. 3(b). From Fig. 3(b), it is evident that upon excitation from 250 nm to 360 nm, there is hardly any red shift but intensity increases gradually which describes that maximum absorption of the light occurs at 350 nm. But, at λex > 350, PL peak shifted towards red-edge of emission spectra. This kind of PL nature of MoTe2@C NDs can be seen more clearly through Fig. S6(b), where normalized PL intensities have been shown for different λex. The excitation-dependent PL nature of the as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs can be ascribed to the heterogeneity of the surface defects and poly diversity due to the quantum-size-effect [58,59]. FTIR spectroscopy has been chosen to look over the chemical ambiance of MoTe2@C NDs (Fig. S6(c)). The measurement suggests the presence of OH, CO, OC, OCO, OH dimer and –CH symmetric stretching in the MoTe2@C NDs (Detailed information is given in Supporting Information Data) [56,59–61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of H2O2 by MoTe2@C NDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, we have tried to explore the possibility of as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs based highly selective sensing probe for the detection of H2O2. First of all, we comprehensively studied the optical response of MoTe2@C NDs in the presence of H2O2. Fig. S7 shows the absorption peak of as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs at 264 nm in the absence of H2O2. With the intrusion of H2O2, the peak at 264 nm gets shifted to 259 nm, which confirms the formation of a new compound or change in the surface functional-groups of MoTe2@C NDs. Also, with gradual incorporation of H2O2 into the solution of MoTe2@C NDs, the absorption band suppressed significantly and can be seen by naked eye indicating the attachment of H2O2 continuously. Besides, the quantum yield (QY) of MoTe2@C NDs has been also calculated with respect to anthracene (as reference) and found to be ∼1.79 % (Table S1) which is comparable to other reports also.

Moreover, experiments have been performed to analyze the FL response of the proposed probe upon addition of different concentrations of H2O2 as described in Fig. 4 (a). Interestingly, the FL intensity decreases gradually with the addition of H2O2. Consequently, we examined the change in the fluorescence intensity of MoTe2@C NDs in response to H2O2. Since, as-synthesized NDs exhibits a fluorescence peak at 431 nm (cyan emission) with an excitation wavelength (via laser light) of 350 nm, with the introduction of H2O2, the fluorescence peak appears at the same position but the intensity simply gets quenched. And, with gradual incorporation of H2O2, fluorescence intensity decreases accordingly (see, Fig. 4(a)). Further, the regression coefficient is most prominent (R2 = 0.9986) in the concentration range 80 nM to 230 nM calculated from Stern-Volmer (S-V) plot followed by eqn (i) [62] (Fig. 4(b)). The limit of detection (LOD) has been calculated using eqn. (ii) and found as ∼14.22 nM. These deduced values signify that as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs have very good sensitivity and selectivity headed for H2O2 when compared with earlier reported probes, Table 1 .

where, F0, F, Ks-v, and SD represent PL intensity without H2O2, with H2O2, S-V quenching constant and standard deviation, respectively.

The sensing has been validated by Time-resolved PL spectroscopy (TRPL) as shown in Fig. S8. The TRPL decay curves were found to be best fitted on tri-exponential function following eqn. (iii). This result unveiled the presence of three kind of fluorescent attachments in the MoTe2@C NDs, and suggests that as-synthesized MoTe2@C NDs possess the average lifetime (τav) as 1.82 ns (decay plot A) which is quite longer than MoTe2@C NDs + H2O2 (1.29 ns) (decay plot B). PL life time of MoTe2@C NDs shows the dependence of the H2O2 on the fluorescence. Henceforth, these outcomes corroborate fluorescence-quenching upon addition of H2O2 to the solution of MoTe2@C NDs.

Since, there is no new peak appeared in the UV–visible absorption spectrum of MoTe2@C NDs after the addition of H2O2, it suggested the PL quenching is purely static. On the other hand, S-V plot is linear, which suggests the dynamic PL quenching. Therefore, it can be concluded that the MoTe2@C NDs + H2O2 comprises both kind of quenching species and causes the both kind of PL quenching phenomena [62–64].

A simplified schematic for the fluorescence-quenching is shown in the Fig. S9. Many researchers were documented that formation of molecular-fluorophore takes place during synthesis and strongly depends upon synthesis method [26,27,65]. Fluorescence-emission rises from the functional group rich surface and core- states of NDs, instead. MoTe2@C NDs, when subjected to UV-light, the supplied-photons facilitates charge transfer phenomenon within the NDs. It can be understanding by simple process, if the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of acceptor sites lower than the LUMO of the donor, photo-excited electron jumps into the LUMO of the acceptor, generating an electron-hole (e−-h+) pair [42]. This process generates a partially-bound e−-h+ pair along with spatially separated two charge carriers known as weakly-bound-exciton. Fluorescence of an exciplex formed by photo-excited electron donor, 1D*, and acceptor 1A (sensitive to applied magnetic-field) in its ground-state helps in spin-conversion, creating geminate radical-ion-pair (RIP, 1,3[D+…A−]), where, superscripts 1 and 3 represent singlet (S = 1) and triplet (S = 3) spin states, respectively. D+ and A− correspond to the charge-transfer-exciton. Photo-excited donor (D*) and electron acceptor (A), in the exciplex 1[D*…A] enables the reversible charge-transfer through contact, which creates geminate RIPs in singlet-state, 1[D*…A] ↔ 1[D+…A−]. Considering hyperfine interaction (HFI), the later one undergoes reversible intersystem crossings to neighboring triplet state 1[D+…A] ↔ 3[D+…A−]. Thus, if 1[D*…A] state remain populated, only then fluorescence can preserve, otherwise quenched [42]. Carboxylic and aromatic (carbon rich) functional-groups attached to the surface of NDs facilitate strong H-bonding due to their high bonding capabilities. However, the equilibrium state, 1[D*···A] ⇌ 1[D+···A−] ↔ 3[D+···A−] and life time can be affected by change in chemical structure and permittivity of the solvents. Apart from it, it has been observed that the fluorescence gets quenched if the relative permittivity of the solvents is quit high leading the strong interactions between the surface functional-groups and solvent, it favors the intersystem crossing process 1[D+···A−] ↔ 3[D+···A−].

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS, FTIR, DLS, Zeta and QY fluctuations on H2O2 sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The surface chemical states of the MoTe2@C NDs in presence of H2O2 was studied through XPS measurement, as shown in Fig. S10. As discussed earlier, the XPS-analysis describes the presence of Mo, Te, O and C in the MoTe2@C NDs, as shown in Fig. 1. After addition of H2O2, no elemental changes were observed. From Fig. 10S(a), Mo 3d spectrum shows that Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 were shifted towards higher binding energy (BE) by 0.6 and 0.2 eV when compared to pristine MoTe2@C NDs. Similarly, for Te (Fig. 10S(b)), 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 shifted towards lower BE by 2.3 and 2.1 eV attributing to the effect of H2O2 on Mo-Te bonds. In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 10S(c)), only sp2 C (which is adventitious) (CC), COH and CO were observed with slight shifting towards lower BE. A functional group OCO was appeared in pristine MoTe2@C NDs, which got dissolved after the addition of H2O2. Further, in O 1s spectrum (Fig. 10S(d)), metallic-O and hydroxyl-groups got prominent and organic CO got suppressed almost, respectively, owing to the strong oxidative nature of H2O2 [13, 45, 46]. Similarly, to observe the relative changes in surface functionalities, FTIR measurement of MoTe2@C NDs in presence of H2O2 has been carried out. The measurement suggests the presence of OH, NC, -CH, COC, CO in the MoTe2@C NDs + H2O2. The findings reveal that few peaks (as appeared in pristine MoTe2@C) got disappeared while few got shifted towards higher wavenumber. Hence, these prominent changes suggest the successful binding of H2O2 with the MoTe2@C [56,59–61]. Also, the changes in the surface morphology of MoTe2@C NDs after the addition of H2O2 can be clearly seen in FTIR spectrum (Fig. S11).

Moreover, DLS measurement reveals the partial agglomeration of MoTe2@C NDs after the addition of H2O2. Hence after the addition of H2O2 three species were found with different hydrodynamic sizes (see Fig. S12(a)), which can be attributed to separation of lightly aggregated NDs. Also, Zeta potential measurement reveal the surface potential values of −1.57 mV for MoTe2@C + H2O2 (Fig. S12(b)). This indicates that the MoTe2@C NDs loses its negative charge. This happened probably due to the colloidal volatility and agglomeration initiated by invalidation of electrostatic stabilization [53,54]. In presence of H2O2, the QY of MoTe2@C NDs has been estimated as ∼0.04 % which favors the sensing of H2O2 by MoTe2@C NDs, detailed analysis is given in supporting Information (Table S1). Hence, surface modification of MoTe2@C after the addition of H2O2 has been supported by XPS, FTIR, DLS, Zeta and QY measurements.

To investigate the applicability of NDs based H2O2 sensing, cell imaging measurement were carried out. Therefore, first cell viability test was conducted and discussed as follows. T lymphoma cell line HuT-78 (2 × 104 cells/mL) was treated for 24 h with different concentrations of MoTe2@C NDs. Further, the viability activity of HuT-78 cells was assessed by Trypan blue dye exclusion assays. No significant viability change was observed in the HuT-78 cells treated with different concentrations of MoTe2@C NDs while compared with the control group Fig. S13(a). Next, HuT-78 (2 × 104 cells/mL) was treated for 24 h with different concentrations of MoTe2@C NDs. Further, the cytotoxic activity of HuT-78 cells was assessed by MTT assays. No significant cytotoxic change was noted in the HuT-78 cells treated with different concentrations of MoTe2@C NDs when compared with the control group Fig. S13(b).

Since, we did not observe any cytotoxic effect of MoTe2@C NDs so we just want to be sure to see the morphological changes in HuT-78 cells at higher doses of 100 µg/mL. To assess morphological changes for viability we treat HuT-78 (2 × 104 cells/mL) cells with 100 µg/mL of MoTe2@C NDs and perform a trypan blue dye assay. Trypan blue stain dead cell but not the live cells. We did not find any significant viability change while compared with its untreated part as no dead cells were found (Fig. S14(a) and (b)). To confirm the morphological cytotoxic changes in HuT-78 cells, cells were stained with Wright and Giemsa stain and observed for the same under the microscope. Wright and Giemsa stain mark the apoptotic cells or dying as their membrane blebbed out. We noted all the cells in both the group MoTe2@C NDs treated and untreated were healthy and live and did not show any significant changes due to MoTe2@C NDs treatment even at the higher concentration treatment of 100 µg/mL MoTe2@C NDs (Fig. S14(c) and (d)).

As we have observed the kinetics of formation of the non-fluorescent compound with the gradual addition of H2O2 concentration, the formation of a non-fluorescent compound is rapid and consistent with higher concentrations of H2O2. This might be due to the strong reactive nature of H2O2 enabling the complex formation or change in chemical structure with MoTe2@C NDs [31]. As discussed in previous sections of this work, the PL of MoTe2@C NDs arises from the surface states and the PL quenching is related to the availability of the molecular fluorophore. A plausible mechanism for the attachment of H2O2 and fluorescence-quenching of MoTe2@C NDs has been illustrated in Fig. 5 . Here, we suggested a three-level-kinetic model [66]. In which, the Singlet-Triplet (S-T) equilibrium of the [1D+…A−] ↔ [3D+…A−] (charge-separated state) corresponds to a trap-state ([D+…A−]trap) along with unidentified multiplicity (Fig. 5(a)), where, f and b represent the reversible exchange between the [1D*…A−] and [D+…A−] trap-states. According to model, trapping-states have relatively distinct energies with respect to [1D*…A−]. TRPL measurements shows that the proposed kinetic model is capable of reproducing the observed results for NDs without and with H2O2. Two important predictions can be made depending upon fluorescence-quenching and TRPL measurement experiments. First, the energy of the trapping state for NDs and NDs + H2O2 are different, and lays above and below with respect to [1D*…A], respectively. This signifies that the NDs and NDs + H2O2 might have un-identical stabilization states of [1D*…A−] owing to distinct polarity or unlike equilibrium among 1,3[D+…A−]. Another one is, TRPL decay plot shows the faster decay in case of NDs + H2O2 with respect to NDs, which reflects the difference in magnitude of non-radiative decay constant corroborating the fluorescence-quenching process. This represents that MoTe2@C and MoTe2@C + H2O2 both has different stabilization states. As discussed earlier, the addition of H2O2 to the NDs affects life time, indicating that the fluorescence emission originates from the surface functionalities and-quenching process is referred to the presence of fluorophores. In Fig. 5, (a) represents a common Jablonski-diagram depicting equilibrium between S-T charge-separated states and 5 (b) refers to the fluorescence -quenching when H2O2 added to the solution of MoTe2@C NDs. In Fig. 5, notation kr, knr and kx refer to the radiative, no-radiative decay rate constant for emitting state and non-radiative decay rate constant for trap states, respectively.

Thus, we have successfully synthesized MoTe2@C NDs for the very first time through a novel and simple ‘Hydrothermal-approach’ following the “bottom-up” strategy. XPS, Raman, XRD and TEM-analyses confirm the formation of MoTe2@C NDs. TEM study reveals the uniform size distribution of MoTe2@C NDs and among most reposes with average-size ∼5.5 nm. Tremendous optical properties of MoTe2@C NDs have been featured by the interpretation of their photophysical properties. The intense cyan fluorescent emission attributes to the surface functional-groups and core-state of MoTe2@C NDs. Moreover, the optical properties have been subjected to examine a MoTe2@C NDs based turn-off fluorescent probe for sensing the H2O2 molecules and, quenching mechanism elucidated. As a result, MoTe2@C NDs can efficiently and selectively sense the H2O2 with a fast response time. The successful synthesis of MoTe2@C NDs and fluorescence-quenching mechanism encourage the fabrication of fluorescence based sensing probes leading potential alternative to detect H2O2 in various biological species.
